June 2026 Agentic AI

The Human Layer

On the function that's missing from most agentic deployments.

This continues some thoughts from The Human Premium, where I wrote about what happens when AI amplifies capability versus dependency.

I've been thinking about a number that showed up in a survey last month: 88% of enterprise leaders said they wish they'd invested more in their data and content foundations before deploying AI agents. Not during. Not after. Before.

That's a remarkably specific regret. And I think it points to something that's going to become increasingly obvious over the next year or two—that the limiting factor on agentic AI isn't the models or the orchestration frameworks or any of the things that get discussed at conferences. It's whether the information those agents depend on actually reflects reality.

I keep running into versions of the same problem. A company deploys an agent to help with something—sales prep, customer support, content generation, whatever—and the agent works exactly as designed. It retrieves information from the systems it's connected to. It synthesizes. It produces outputs that look reasonable. And those outputs are wrong in ways that nobody catches until they've already caused damage, because the agent was grounded in data that was stale, incomplete, or just never accurate in the first place.

The technology worked. The information didn't.

The framework I keep coming back to

I've started thinking about agentic deployments in terms of three layers that all have to work together. The first is orchestration—how agents coordinate, hand off tasks, manage workflows. This is where most of the vendor energy goes, and it's genuinely hard engineering. The second is grounding—what agents actually know, how they retrieve information, how they stay connected to sources of truth. This has gotten a lot of attention recently as RAG and knowledge graphs have become standard architecture.

The third layer is what I'd call strategic oversight, and it's the one almost nobody has. It's the ongoing function of making sure that what agents know actually reflects what's true about your customers and your business. Not as a one-time data quality project, but as a continuous responsibility that someone owns.

When I talk to companies about their agentic deployments, the first two layers are usually in decent shape. They've got the platforms, the integrations, the retrieval pipelines. What's missing is anyone who owns the question of whether the information flowing through those pipelines is actually trustworthy.

Why this falls between chairs

The reason this function doesn't exist is that it doesn't fit cleanly into any existing role.

The data team owns data infrastructure, but their focus is typically inward—governance, compliance, pipeline reliability. They're not spending time in customer conversations validating whether the CRM data reflects reality. That's not their job.

Sales owns the customer relationship, but they're focused on closing deals. They're not thinking about whether the systems that feed AI agents are capturing the nuances of what's happening in their accounts. They update the CRM when they have to, with whatever level of detail gets them to the next stage.

Marketing owns messaging and positioning, but they're thinking about campaigns. They're not considering how their content gets consumed by AI systems that will represent the company in contexts they never anticipated.

Product owns the roadmap, but they're thinking about features, not about how product information propagates through AI systems and gets synthesized into customer-facing recommendations that may or may not reflect what the product actually does.

Everyone owns a piece of the information that agents depend on. Nobody owns the question of whether it's true.

"The technology worked. The information didn't."

What the work actually looks like

If this function existed—and I think it will have to exist, eventually—what would the person doing it actually spend their time on?

Some of it would be what you might call translation work. Spending time with customers and with customer-facing teams, understanding what's actually happening in those relationships, and making sure that understanding gets encoded in ways that AI systems can use. There's an enormous amount of tacit knowledge in any organization—things salespeople know about accounts, things support teams know about common problems, things product people know about how customers actually use features versus how they're supposed to use them. Most of that knowledge never makes it into systems. It lives in people's heads, gets shared in conversations, and never becomes available to AI agents that could use it.

Some of it would be what I'd call auditing. Taking what agents produce, comparing it to what's actually true, and when there's a gap—which there usually is—tracing that gap back to its source. When an agent gives a sales rep a bad briefing, the problem isn't usually the model. It's somewhere in the information supply chain, and someone needs to find it and fix it.

Some of it would be architecture work. As the business changes—new products, new customer segments, new competitive dynamics—the information structures that ground AI agents have to evolve alongside. This isn't a one-time setup. It's an ongoing responsibility.

And some of it would be governance work. As agents take on more responsibility, someone needs to think carefully about where the boundaries should be. Which decisions should agents make on their own? Which need human review? How do you keep humans in the loop for the moments that actually matter? These are design questions, and they require someone who understands both the customer relationship and the agent capabilities well enough to draw reasonable lines.

The CDP connection

I wrote a book about customer data platforms a few years ago, and the central argument was that enterprises needed a unified view of customer data to deliver coherent experiences. That argument hasn't gone away, but the shape of the problem has changed.

It's not enough to unify the data anymore. The data has to be true. It has to be current. It has to reflect what's actually happening with customers, not the version of events that made it through the filters of CRM update compliance and quarterly business review preparation. And crucially, someone has to be accountable for keeping it true over time.

When I wrote The Human Premium, the argument was that AI amplifies whatever you bring to it—strong foundations get stronger, weak foundations get exposed. That same logic applies at the organizational level. An AI agent is only as good as the information it's grounded in, and the quality of that information is a function of whether anyone is actually responsible for maintaining it.

Where this is heading

I don't know exactly what this function will be called when companies start creating it. It touches data architecture, customer strategy, AI governance, and organizational design all at once. It requires someone who can move between technical systems and customer relationships, who understands both what the data says and what's actually true.

What I do know is that the 88% regret number is going to get worse before it gets better. Companies are deploying agents faster than they're fixing the information foundations those agents depend on. The agents will keep working as designed, producing outputs that look authoritative, and the gap between what they say and what's true will keep growing until someone is accountable for closing it.

The companies that figure this out will have agents that actually know their customers. The companies that don't will have agents that confidently make things up. The difference won't be in the models they use or the platforms they deploy. It'll be in whether anyone owns the truth.
